title: **Balance Sheet Overview – As of March 31, 2026**
content:
DATA CHECK: The source table is labeled 'As of December 31, 2026' at the bottom right. However, the column headers for percentage changes explicitly reference a comparison to 'Previous Quarter'. A balance sheet dated March 31 would not have data available from that future date; therefore, the figures provided in the columns are interpreted as being relative to the most recent prior quarter ending December 31, 2025. The narrative below uses these specific percentage changes against their respective previous periods.

The Balance Sheet Overview – As of March 31, 2026
Total assets stood at $10,493 million as of the period end, increased 1.6% compared to the prior quarter.
The largest asset category grew significantly during this time; Loans and Leases rose to $6,628 million, up 0.2%. Investment securities also expanded slightly, reaching $2,204 million, an increase of 2.7%, which added a larger base of earning assets for the institution.
Cash and deposits grew substantially over this period, ending at $81,7 million, increased 12.9% from the prior quarter position. Other Assets moved more modestly as well, finishing the period at $83,7 million after an increase of 0.7%. The remaining smaller categories together shifted slightly, with other investments holding steady.
Loans and Leases is now the largest component of the balance sheet. That growth on the asset side was funded primarily through shares/deposits, reflecting how the institution's holdings were paid for during the period.
Total liabilities stood at $8,506 million, increased 1.3% from the prior quarter, led by accounts payable and accrued interest which rose to $3,7 million, up 40.9%. Shares/deposits also grew over this time frame to reach $8,455 million, an increase of 1.2%, while other liabilities moved more modestly.
Total equity stood at $87,8 million, increased 1.8% from the prior quarter position. Taken together, the period saw a net expansion in total assets funded by member deposits and supported by growth in retained capital. Management continues to watch the pace of loan growth relative to deposit growth.
